speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 18, 1995. This video is about Jewish art and artifacts in Dr. Otto Schneid's collection called, It will be presented by Rosanne Krusner, camera by Anthony Breier. We're in Toronto, Canada, and the language of the video is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Otto Naftali Schneid was born January 30, 1900 in Jablunkova, Czechoslovakia, a small town in the Czech Beskid Mountains, but at an early age moved with his family to Bielitz, Bielsko Silesia, in the Polish Beskid Mountains. Born into a distinguished and learned Jewish family, Otto trained as an art historian and received his PhD from University of Vienna in 1926. In 1930, Otto began work on an encyclopedia of Jewish art, collecting photographs and correspondence of his contemporary Jewish artists throughout Europe. In 1933, when Hitler declared Jewish art as degenerate, Otto decided to alter his encyclopedia in order to create a book on Jewish artists for Hitler to understand that, in fact, Jewish art is not degenerate art. In addition, between 1936 and 1938, Schneid established and curated an art museum in Vilna. The museum was later destroyed by the Nazis. In 1938, Dr. Schneid's manuscript was presented to the Viennese publisher and awaiting print, when Austria was invaded. The manuscript was confiscated by the Nazis, and Otto was put on the blacklist. An Austrian citizen, Otto had been hiding in Warsaw for six months and eventually fell into the hands of the Polish police, who were planning to extradite him. Three days after his arrest and incarceration, Otto was released. The Jewish public outcry had been so strong that the Polish police had to release Dr. Schneid. However, because Otto was still on the blacklist, he had to leave Europe. He was given a scholar's visa to study in the Hebrew University in Jerusalem. And in 1939, Otto left for Palestine with a copy of the manuscript and photographs he had kept. Between 1939 and 1945, Otto earned a meager living by lecturing on kibbutzim and writing books. In 1945, he met and married Miriam nee Goldshmidt, a poet 24 years his junior. In 1946, Otto discovered the fate of his family. His mother Bluma, sister Genia, and brother-in-law Hugo Hamburger had been murdered in Auschwitz on Tisha BA'v 1943. After this news, Otto's thoughts, writings, paintings and sculpture all became imbued with Holocaust and biblical iconography. In the early 1950s, Otto decided to translate into Hebrew and update his book on Jewish artists, and to see what became of the artists and their families. The book would become, therefore, a book of remembrance. In the late 1950s, the manuscript was once again about to go into print in Israel when Cecil Roth's book on Jewish art was released. The publisher, not wanting to flood the market, chose not to print Otto's book and the book was never published. Otto died in Toronto, Canada in 1974. After his death, Miriam, now remarried to rabbi Dr. Arieh L. Ofseyer, tried to place in an institution Otto's collection of photographs and correspondence with these Jewish artists, many of whom were lost in the Holocaust. In the process, the collections was designated a national treasure by the Canadian government. Otto's was never placed, and remains today in Miriam's possession. This video looks briefly at Otto's oeuvre, Hitler's conception of degenerate art, the question of what is Jewish art, and focuses in on some representative pieces from In the 1950s and '60s, Otto exhibited many of his works internationally. Some of his pieces are in the FDR Memorial Library, Hyde Park, New York, USA. The Palazzo Pitti, Florence, Italy. The Royal Ontario Museum, Toronto, Canada. The British Museum London, England. And the Albertina in Vienna. The following are examples of Otto Schneid's oeuvre. The first painting is from 1966. It's an oil on panel, and the size is 63 inches by 25 and 1/2 inches. The theme of this painting is loneliness and the gaps created by death. The title of this painting is It was painted in the late 1950s. It is pastel on paper. The size is 34 inches by 25 and 1/2 inches. This is a Holocaust painting. The title of this painting is It was painted in the 1970s. It's oil on canvas. The size is 36 inches by 25 inches. is part of the series. It's a turn-able painting, meaning it can be hung on any of its four sides. The turn-able painting is an innovation of Otto Schneid. The title of this painting is It's another part of the series. It was painted in the 1970s. It's oil on canvas, and the size is 36 inches by 25 inches. This is also a turn-able painting. Turn-able paintings also can be hung on the ceiling or on the floor. painted in 1963. The medium is oil on panel. The size is 41 inches by 30 and 1/2 inches. The motif is part of Otto Schneid's human art, a theme he returned to throughout his life. 1960. Oil on canvas. 32 inches by 36 inches. This is another of Otto Schneid's human art paintings. A man is lying on the street while everyone walks away. 1960. Oil on canvas. 38 inches by 51 inches. A biblical painting. The angel's hands symbolize conflict from within oneself. The model was Otto's son, Jacob. 1961. Oil on canvas. 51 by 40 inches. A biblical painting. The fiery horses carry Eliyahu to heaven, while his student Elisha watches from the earth below. The title of this painting is It was painted in the late 1960s. It's oil on canvas, and the size is 40 by 30 inches. A Holocaust painting, and example of human art. The man stands without shoes in an empty world. The tree on the right is as lonely and devastated as he is. 1961. Oil on canvas board. The size is 24 by 30 inches. Attics were hiding places during the Holocaust. What we see here is an attic from before the war. The title of this work is It was painted in 1965, and is oil on masonite. The size is 27 by 48 inches. Otto called this his most optimistic painting. The skeleton smiles while people exit through the gates. The walking figures are Jews leaving from Russia to Israel. To get a feeling of earth, Otto mixed sand into the paint he used to illustrate the skeleton. is a prophetic work. As mentioned, the painting was completed in 1965, many years before the Jews of Russia actually were permitted to leave en masse. For more information on the life and art of Dr. Otto Naftali Schneid, please refer to the interview with Miriam Schneid-Ofseyer in the collection of the recorded in Toronto, Canada, August 8, 1995. One of Hitler's tactics of demonstrating the superiority of the Aryan race was by demonstrating the superiority of true German art over what the Nazis referred to as degenerate, Bolshevik, and Jewish art. In 1931, Hitler had stated that he would release a tornado against modern art. And two years later, Nazi propaganda decisively declared all artistic productions of this sort must be burned. In is 1935 speech at Nuremberg, Hitler said, 'One will no longer discuss or deal with these corrupters of art. They are fools, liars or criminals who belong in insane asylums or prisons.' Then in 1937, shortly after he staged the Nazi approved Great Exhibition of German Art, Hitler attended the politically organized and now infamous Degenerate Art Exhibition in Munich. The material making up the show was part of nearly 20,000 works of modern art that had been confiscated from German museums by the orders of Joseph Goebbels. Against this political backdrop, we can appreciate Dr. Schneid's efforts and venture. Even before the 1937 exhibition, it was in response to Hitler's ideas on art that Dr. Schneid decided to put into print a book on Jewish art to explain to the Nazis that Jewish art is not degenerate.

speaker: interviewer
text: The question remains. What is Jewish art? How do we define it? Should we define it? Should we and do we differentiate between a Jewish artist and an artist who happens to be Jewish? Modigliani, for instance, used to sell his drawings to tourists in Montparnasse, with the introduction Modigliani, [NON-ENGLISH]. Yet despite the fact that he fully disclosed his ancestry, critics never really paid much mind to the reality that he was, indeed, a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: The artists who responded to Dr. Schneid's call for submissions were all Jews. However, the subject matter and styles of their work greatly differ. In you will see scenes of everyday Jewish life, traditional academic art, avant-garde experimental ism, and even the doll-maker's craft. Some, like [INAUDIBLE], consider their Judaism a burden. Some considered it a footnote in their lives. And still others refer to themselves, point blank, as Jewish artists. Regardless of their mindset or style, in Hitler's Nazi conception, simply by the fact that they were Jews, their artistic endeavors ranked as hideous, irrational, and degenerate. Here now, in no particular order, are some of the 167 men and women represented in Dr. Schneid's Some names are familiar, some are not. Some survived World War II, some did not.
